topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the shared lab hummed with an irritating, monotonous drone. Elias stared at the blueprint spread across the steel table, his jaw tight enough to crack enamel. He traced a line with a fingertip, then pulled back, his gaze flicking to the adjacent workstation. Across the narrow aisle, Maya was meticulously aligning the same schematic, her brow furrowed in concentration. A cold knot began to tighten in Elias’s chest, a heavy, suffocating pressure that made his breath catch in his throat. He’d spent the last three weeks wrestling with this design, pouring every waking hour into perfecting the flux capacitor schematic. Now, seeing her identical progress, a strange sense of disorientation washed over him. He pushed his chair back abruptly, the scrape of metal against the floor sounding deafening in the quiet space. He needed air.

[story 2]

“It’s impossible,” whispered Lena, her voice barely audible above the whir of the cooling fans. She leaned in close to the monitor displaying the complex algorithmic structure, her knuckles white where she gripped the edge of the desk. Her eyes darted from the code on her screen to the identical lines displayed on the adjacent terminal belonging to Ben. A tremor ran through her hands, making the keyboard tremble slightly. For weeks, she had been convinced she was the only one tackling this particular problem—the optimization sequence for the quantum entanglement matrix. The realization that Ben had been working on the exact same iteration, the exact same breakthrough, sent a dizzying wave through her. She pressed her palms against her eyes, trying to force the image to dissolve, but the symmetry of their efforts mocked her composure.

[story 3]

I found myself pacing the perimeter of the workshop, the worn linoleum cool beneath my worn boots. Every time I glanced toward the corner where Alex was hunched over his console, a distinct hollowness opened up inside me. It wasn't anger, not exactly; it was a deep, unsettling vacancy, as if the very foundation of my own achievement had suddenly shifted. The project—the intricate lattice design for the new energy conduit—was mine, a solitary endeavor I’d nurtured with obsessive focus. Yet, seeing Alex’s hand move across his interface, replicating my precise sequence of inputs, made my stomach clench. I stopped pacing, planting my feet firmly. I felt a sudden, overwhelming need to check my own work, to verify that the anomaly wasn't just a trick of the light, but a genuine, horrifying duplication.
